quoteLast: a canoe, the first which they had seen, came staggering toward them under a huge three-cornered sail. As it came near, they could see two Indians on board.
title: Westward Ho! Or, The Voyages and Adventures of Sir Amyas Leigh, Knight, of Burrough, in the County of Devon, in the Reign of Her Most Glorious Majesty Queen Elizabeth
author: Kingsley, Charles, 1819-1875

quoteFirst: Exercising caution lest I should be watched, I had immediately on arrival telephoned from my hotel bedroom to Lydford, but the response came back in a woman's voice that "the master" and Miss Asta were still abroad.  Therefore
quoteLast: on the morning following my return I went round to Bedford Row in a taxi, and was quickly shown into the sombre private room of an elderly, quiet-spoken man--Mr George Napier, head of the firm.
title: The Lost Million
author: Le Queux, William, 1864-1927

quoteFirst: Nevertheless, Andrews and Wiswall selected this latter route, and the third day out from our camp reached the old abandoned stage station at Crow Flat
quoteLast: . This was in an open country and from it one could see for miles in every direction. A cold north wind was blowing, so, for protection, the two men drove inside the old station walls, unhitched and hobbled their horses and pony and were soon busily baking bread, frying bacon and boiling coffee, not dreaming there was an Indian in the country, though they had been warned to look out for them.
title: Six Years with the Texas Rangers, 1875 to 1881
author: Gillett, James B., 1856-1937

quoteLast: we ran on to a sandbank, but as the tide was rising this did not much matter; in fact, it was rather convenient, as we were thus enabled to have a meal on a _steady_ boat, a matter of importance to some of our party.
title: Head-hunters, black, white, and brown
author: Haddon, Alfred C. (Alfred Cort), 1855-1940

quoteFirst: We passed by two or three large hills of a deep red color, that seemed to be recently thrown up by an eruption. Our path lay over rough heaps of broken lava, where a horse could not have gone without stumbling at every step, yet our sure-footed beasts carried us safely over the most difficult spots.
quoteLast: , the sky, which had hitherto been clear, began to grow overcast, and I could perceive that the smoke from the crater, instead of streaming off to the northeast, was now rolling down the side of the mountain directly toward us. This showed that the wind had shifted to the north, and I felt serious apprehensions when I observed the increasing blackness of the sky.
title: Robert Merry's Museum, Volumes I-II (1841)
author: Various; Goodrich, Samuel G. (Samuel Griswold), 1793-1860 [Editor]

quoteFirst: We came in sight of the coast of South America
quoteLast: , and dropped anchor in the harbour of Bahia at four P.M.; and about half an hour after I went on shore with Mr. Lushington, a person of the name of Wilson taking us in his boat: there was a slave in the boat, and, not knowing that he understood English, I asked Mr.
title: Journals of Two Expeditions of Discovery in North-West and Western Australia, Volume 1
author: Grey, George, 1812-1898

quoteFirst: SIR:——In compliance with your written request of yesterday, I will state that Captain Jackson will leave this post
quoteLast: to-day, with about thirty men; will be at Link river to-night, and I hope before morning at Captain Jack’s camp.
title: Wigwam and war-path; or, The royal chief in chains
author: Meacham, A. B. (Alfred Benjamin), 1826-1882

quoteLast: the schooner dropped anchor, and Barringford and Dave went ashore with the lieutenant and walked up to Fort Detroit. They were allowed to enter without question, and soon found themselves in the presence of Major Gladwyn.
title: The fort in the wilderness
author: Stratemeyer, Edward, 1862-1930; Shute, A. B. (Augustus Burnham), 1851-1906 [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: The palms shielded him and shaded him and he got himself into such a corner that no one could approach him from behind or see him without being seen. And all that morning he neither heard nor saw aught worthy of mark until
quoteLast: a voice in the ship cried out so that Jacob could plainly understand the words, "One should watch from land. Now a man on the hilltop could serve us well."
title: The Dark Frigate
author: Hawes, Charles Boardman, 1889-1923; Fischer, Anton Otto, 1882-1962 [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: But the day was to be more eventful even than the promise of the morning had suggested. And the second surprise came
title: The Twins of Suffering Creek
author: Cullum, Ridgwell, 1867-1943
